title: Fix to generalize the canSkipClobberingStore
author: Bhuvan1527
description:
Resolves: [#173905](https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/issues/173905)

### Output of -O3 before the change:
```
define dso_local i32 @set(ptr noundef captures(none) initializes((0, 4)) %0, ptr noundef writeonly captures(none) initializes((0, 4)) %1, i32 noundef %2) local_unnamed_addr #0 {
  store i32 %2, ptr %0, align 4
  store i32 %2, ptr %1, align 4 
  %4 = load i32, ptr %0, align 4
  %5 = add i32 %4, %2
  ret i32 %5
}
```
### Output of -O3 After the change (Want to achieve):
```
; Function Attrs: mustprogress nofree noinline norecurse nosync nounwind willreturn memory(argmem: write) uwtable
define dso_local noundef range(i32 0, -1) i32 @set(ptr noundef writeonly captures(none) initializes((0, 4)) %0, ptr noundef writeonly captures(none) initializes((0, 4)) %1, i32 noundef %2) local_unnamed_addr #0 {
  store i32 %2, ptr %0, align 4
  store i32 %2, ptr %1, align 4
  %4 = shl i32 %2, 1
  ret i32 %4
}
```
### Issue: 

- The extra **load Inst** was retained by the GVNPass because during it's `processLoad()`, the memory dependency analysis(`canSkipClobberingStore()`) has decided that the load is dependent on the 2nd store. 
 
### Reason:

- Current code of canSkipClobberingStore is only skipping the store, if its value operand is an output of a load instruction. That is it is specific.
- This pr is an attempt to generalize this condition and include if the operand is a constant or a function argument.

### Code Change:

- If the ValueOperand of the store is a function argument, and the store is the first instruction where the pointerOperand is used. Then we ...

comments.author: Bhuvan1527
comments.body:
> This is very obviously incorrect. Please read the comment above the function and try to understand what this code is actually doing. Then look at the code in the issue and try to understand why it's valid to optimize that code.
> 
> (Hint: Everything here hinges on "writes the same value that MemLoc contains now". The value being an argument tells you absolutely nothing about the current value of the location.)

Yes @nikic . I totally understand what this function is for. The patch is just to verify the fix. 
I'm thinking of bypassing the store if it is storing there for the first time. (but that is also like change at the memory location).
